text:
Spectators and security clashed at theOlympicsafter a woman held up a banner in support of Taiwan.

The incident occurred at a badminton match between Denmark and Taiwan on Friday.

Taiwan have sent 60 athletes toParisbut are forced to compete under the name ‘Chinese Taipei’ as the result of a political compromise withChina. As part of the agreement, Taiwan are not able to compete using its own name, flag or anthem.

During the badminton, a woman stood on the steps inside the stadium and held up a large green banner in the shape of Taiwan with gold lettering which read: ‘Let’s go, Taiwan’.

A member of security began to approach her but he then began speaking into his walkie-talkie. A different man then walked in front of the woman and stood in front of the banner in an apparent attempt to block it from view.

The woman then repositioned and held the banner up even higher. The man then angrily ripped the banner from her hands and began to walk up the stairs to the exit before the security guard grabbed him and escorted him out.

Liberty Times reports that a woman’s “Taiwan Add Oil” banner was snatched by Chinese national during a badminton match at the Paris Olympics. Video via social media.pic.twitter.com/494z4L9aSR

Before that clash, the same security guard snatched another green banner which read, ‘Taiwan’, from the hands of a man who was sitting down.

As the security guard walked back up the steps with the confiscated Taiwan banner he was booed and jeered by supporters who were sat in the vicinity.

yo@OlympicsItâs a pity enough that, as Taiwanese, we can’t bring our national flag to the games. From what I understand, the tower that got dragged off from the audience doesnât show the word âTaiwanâ, yet it got taken away like itâs illegal to support our own country. like??pic.twitter.com/yOJusSFodz

Taiwan’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs said it ‘strongly condemns the crude and despicable means of malicious individuals ruthlessly snatching the ‘Go Taiwan’ slogan’.

The ministry added: ‘This violent act is not only uneducated, but also seriously violates the civilized spirit represented by the Olympic Games. It also violates the rule of law and infringes on freedom of speech.’

China and Taiwan have been governed separately since they split in 1949 following a civil war.

Asked about the incident on Saturday, the International Olympic Committee’s (IOC) spokesman, Mark Adams, said that the conditions of entry to Olympic venues have been made clear to spectators on each ticket.

The terms and conditions of entry allow fans to only display flags of countries and territories participating in the games and forbids any banners that display political messages.

title: Fans clash at Olympics as man rips Taiwan banner out of woman's hands | Metro News
description: Spectators and security clashed at the Olympics after a woman held up a banner in support of Taiwan.
